title: Beach Hut Bed
summary: A wild kids' loft bed shaped like two peaked beach huts joined by a raised center floor, with siding, a shingled roof, and a ladder. A big, ambitious 2x4 build.
description:
This is a showpiece kids' bed: two peaked-roof 'huts' with clapboard siding and a boarded roof, joined by a raised center platform with a lip, tied together by a ladder — the whole thing four feet tall at the center and roofed like a little village. It is by far one of the largest and most involved builds in the catalog, framed from 20 sheets' worth of 2x4 and 1x4, and it needs real shop space and, ideally, help.

The backbone is four identical roof trusses. Build all four exactly alike and dead square — every later panel references them, and a truss that's off throws the roofline out visibly. Two of the trusses get an extra top 2x4 (the two outside ones). The huts are then two truss-pairs each, sided and roofed.

A practical reality runs through the whole plan: doorways. Assembled hut frames are 26" wide, siding adds 3/4", and the roof boards run 30-1/4" wide — so at several steps the plan warns you to check whether the growing assembly still fits through your doorway, and if not, to do that step in the final room. The center floor and huts are bolted together with 4-1/2" lag bolts (four per side), which you must periodically re-check and re-tighten. The plan is candid that the assembled huts are top-heavy and tippy until fully joined, and it explicitly says to add back cross-bracing for stability and to beef up the top if you have a climber. Build and use with real care — this is a tall structure a child sleeps in and will absolutely climb on.

tools.note: Endless 45° truss and siding cuts — a stop block is essential.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Cross braces, ladder rungs, back support.

materials.name: Lag bolts, 4-1/2 in, with nuts and washers

cutList.note: Short-point; one end 45° off square. Overall 72".
One end 45° off square, long-point measurement.

steps.title: Build four matching trusses
steps.body: Build four roof trusses exactly alike, using 2-1/2" pocket screws or countersunk 3" screws with glue. Make sure all four match exactly and are square — every panel from here references them, so a truss that's off shows up in the whole roofline.

steps.title: Add the extra top on the two outside trusses
steps.body: On the two outside trusses only, attach another 2x4 flush to the top of the legs, flush to the outside like the lower support. Only two of the four trusses get this.

steps.title: Build the two hut frames
steps.body: First check you can move a 26"-wide assembly through your doorway — if not, assemble in the final room. Build two hut frames by joining an end truss and an open truss into each. Build two identical huts.

steps.title: Add the side supports and floors
steps.body: Use the pocket-hole jig to drill the side supports and attach them to the trusses; the window openings are at the top, so adjust their placement as you like. Add the plywood hut floors and the floor joists/supports beneath.

steps.title: Fit the cross braces
steps.body: Lay the cross braces over the sides of the bottom and mark them, then remove and cut the ends at an angle so they seat as shown. Make sure everything is very square before you screw them down — the braces lock the hut's geometry.

steps.title: Side the huts
steps.body: If your doorway was tight, side in the final room — siding adds 3/4". Lay out the siding with ~1/4" gaps, screwing with 2" screws (removable) or 2" finish nails and glue (permanent), keeping everything square. This stiffens the huts a lot, but they're now top-heavy and tippy — be cautious. Then add the side siding, which you can nail for speed and easier hole concealment.

steps.title: Roof the huts
steps.body: The roof runs 30-1/4" wide — if that won't fit through your doorway, roof in the room. Start the roofing at the peak and work down. Unlike the siding, the roofing boards butt tight with no gaps. Both huts are now done.

steps.title: Build the center floor and trim
steps.body: Construct the center floor with 3" screws and glue, adjusting for square before screwing the plywood to the frame. Attach the 2x6 center trim to the sides, three screws per board, keeping the 2x6 flush to the bottom so there's a lip at the top to keep things inside.

steps.title: Bolt the center to the huts
steps.body: With all pieces in the final room, attach the center platform to the two huts with the 4-1/2" lag bolts, washers, and nuts — predrill the holes, four bolts per side. These bolts hold the whole bed together; check them periodically and re-tighten as needed.
materials: Lag bolts, 4-1/2 in, with nuts and washers

steps.title: Add the ladder, top, back support, and finish
steps.body:
Add the ladder rungs — this ties the two huts together at the base; bolt or screw them. Add the 2x2 center top to hold the huts in position up high (beef it up if you have a climber). Add the 1x3 back support, and if there's any wiggle, add back cross-bracing like the sides to take it out.

Sand and finish. Note the bed is 4 ft tall at center without a mattress — you fold the mattress, slide it through the opening, and let it unfold inside. Build and use carefully.

images.alt: Children's loft bed built to look like two peaked beach huts with a roof and ladder
